fact: In the Northern Hemisphere, this is when squirrels typically start getting cranky because they're running low on their buried winter snacks.
The sun rises about 30 minutes later on January 31 than it did on New Year's Day in most places - winter is still winning the daylight battle!
January 31 often falls in the middle of 'mud season' in many cold places, when snow melts during the day but freezes again at night, creating slippery ice patches everywhere.
This date marks exactly one month since the winter solstice, which means northern regions have typically gained back about 45 minutes of daylight.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, January 31 is peak summer - cicadas are usually buzzing so loudly they can drown out conversations!
January 31 is when many deciduous trees in cold climates have their deepest winter sleep - their sap moves so slowly it's almost like they're frozen solid inside.
January 31 is still in Aquarius season - you have to wait until February 19 for Pisces to show up in the zodiac.
In northern regions, January 31 typically marks when owls start hooting more at night because they're beginning to think about finding mates for spring.
This is when many evergreen trees like pine and spruce usually have their needles at their darkest green color - they've been storing up energy all winter long.
January 31 sits exactly 49 days before the spring equinox, making it a halfway point between deep winter and the return of equal day and night.
In snowy places, January 31 is when you might see the weirdest animal tracks - desperate rabbits, foxes, and deer often create crazy zigzag patterns while searching for food.
